# UC-22.2.27 · NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Beginner &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Security &middot; **Wave:** Walk &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We measure how quickly real people answer when someone emails a security weakness, so you can show the world you treat those messages seriously instead of letting them gather dust.*

---

## Description

Aggregates ServiceNow incidents tagged for coordinated disclosure and calculates what share missed a 48-hour work-start SLA, producing breach rate and average triage hours for leadership review.

## Value

Researchers and regulators increasingly ask for proof you respond quickly; this search turns mailbox chaos into two numbers—percent late and average hours—suitable for an annual assurance letter without hand-counting tickets.

## Implementation

(1) Normalize `category` values in SNOW so disclosure incidents always match `*Vulnerability Disclosure*`. (2) Map `work_start` or `work_notes` first activity timestamp consistently — adjust `strptime` format to your locale. (3) Use HEC Ack for bug bounty ingestion at `/services/collector/event`. (4) Keep raw email in `index=email_gateway` with PII access restricted via RBAC. (5) Export monthly PDF to `index=audit_evidence`.

## Evidence

Saved search `UC-22.2.27` monthly workbook, ServiceNow incident export hash, and HEC receipt IDs archived under the vulnerability disclosure control folder.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Import fixture `snow:incident` rows where half the tickets never receive `work_start` within 48 hours of `opened_at`; the summary panel shows `breach_pct` above zero and `avg_triage` above the policy line.

### Negative scenario

Replay fixtures where every incident records `work_start` within six hours and categories strictly follow the disclosure naming standard; the search shows `breach_pct` equal to zero while ticket volume remains non-zero.

## Detailed Implementation

## Prerequisites

- **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
- **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
- **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
- **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(e)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.27: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


- **Hint 1:** Normalize `category` values in SNOW so disclosure incidents always match `*Vulnerability Disclosure*`. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

- **Hint 2:** Map `work_start` or `work_notes` first activity timestamp consistently — adjust `strptime` format to your locale. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 3:** Use HEC Ack for bug bounty ingestion at `/services/collector/event`. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

- **Hint 4:** Keep raw email in `index=email_gateway` with PII access restricted via RBAC. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 5:** Export monthly PDF to `index=audit_evidence`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `opened_at` (format: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `opened_at` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval opened_at_epoch = strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(opened_at_epoch)
| stats count by opened_at
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(opened_at, "fmt1"), strptime(opened_at, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `opened_at` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the last 90 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.27: NIS2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy Operational Signals]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.27,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `avg_triage`, `breach_pct`, `breaches`, `opened`, `sla_breach`, `tickets`, `triage_hours`, `triaged`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table avg_triage, breach_pct, breaches, opened, sla_breach, tickets, triage_hours, triaged
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `triage_hours`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(triage_hours) as p50 perc90(triage_hours) as p90 perc99(triage_hours) as p99 max(triage_hours) as hi
```

The current threshold `48` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.27: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Single Value Breach Pct (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Line Chart Avg Triage (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Table Of Worst Incidents With Drilldown To Snow (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

- 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

- Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

- Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

- Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.

## SPL

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="*Vulnerability Disclosure*" earliest=-90d
| eval opened=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triaged=strptime(work_start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval triage_hours=round((triaged-opened)/3600,2)
| eval sla_breach=if(triage_hours>48 OR isnull(triaged),1,0)
| stats count as tickets sum(sla_breach) as breaches avg(triage_hours) as avg_triage
| eval breach_pct=round(100*breaches/tickets,1)
| table tickets, breaches, breach_pct, avg_triage
```

## Visualization

Single value breach_pct, line chart avg_triage, table of worst incidents with drilldown to SNOW.

## Known False Positives

• **Major Sev-1 outage steals triage time** — Distinguish: incidents opened during documented crisis window with problem record. Suppress: `crisis_blackout.csv` time-bound exception register matched on opened_at.
• **Holidays or on-call rotation gaps** — Distinguish: no analyst logged `work_start` until Tuesday. Suppress: adjust SLA macro for bank holidays via lookup `holiday_calendar.csv`.
• **Duplicate SNOW records for same researcher email** — Distinguish: two incidents share external reporter hash. Suppress: `dedup number` after sort -_time or merge workflow in ServiceNow.
• **Bugcrowd API lag replays old reports** — Distinguish: `_time` cluster but `opened_at` stale. Suppress: filter HEC events `where payload_age_seconds < 900` at ingest time using **props.conf** `EVAL`.
• **Category typo breaks match** — Distinguish: team renamed category to `Vuln Disclosure`. Suppress: broaden search `where like(category,"%Disclosure%")` or maintain `nis2_vd_categories.csv` lookup.
